original_abstract: Community nursing serves as a fundamental pillar of the Ecuadorian public health system, playing a decisive role in health promotion and disease prevention across both rural and marginalized urban landscapes. This study examines the multifaceted contributions of community nurses, emphasizing their leadership in health education, nationwide immunization initiatives, and the comprehensive management of chronic conditions. Evidence suggests that targeted educational interventions have significantly improved lifestyle behaviors, leading to a marked decrease in the incidence of childhood malnutrition and gastrointestinal infections. Furthermore, community outreach has expanded vaccination coverage in underserved regions and enhanced therapeutic adherence among patients with non-communicable diseases, thereby improving overall quality of life. Despite these successes, the profession grapples with systemic barriers, including precarious infrastructure, chronic resource shortages, and limited access to specialized continuing education. This paper argues that strengthening community nursing in Ecuador requires the integration of innovative digital tools, the development of robust public policies, and the fostering of intersectoral partnerships. Addressing these challenges is essential to maximizing the impact of nursing interventions and achieving more equitable public health outcomes nationwide.
humanized_abstract: This article explores the role of community nursing in Ecuador. Community nursing is a cornerstone of the Ecuadorian public health system and is particularly important in rural and disadvantaged urban areas for the promotion, prevention and control of disease. The study shows that community nurses work closely together with other health professionals, not only to deliver education but also to conduct national vaccination campaigns, to manage chronic conditions and to work together with patients to strengthen their self-care practices. The study highlights how education has had a positive effect, resulting in a significant reduction in stunting and in cases of diarrheal disease. It has also improved access to immunization in disadvantaged regions and improved adherence to treatments among patients with non-communicable diseases, thereby contributing to an improvement in quality of life. Community nurses in Ecuador, however, are facing a number of challenges, including lack of infrastructure, insufficient human and material resources, and access to ongoing training. Therefore, the article highlights the need for digital resources to support community nurses, adequate public policies and intersectoral collaboration to reinforce community nursing in Ecuador, with the ultimate goal of ensuring the optimal application of interventions and a more equable distribution of benefits at the national level.
